name: Appellate Mandate
summary: Drafts a formal appellate mandate that concludes the appeal process and directs the trial court to implement the appellate court's decision. Extracts precise details from the appellate record, opinion, and procedural history to construct the caption, incorporate the judgment, and specify remand directives. Use this skill immediately after an appellate ruling to prepare the official order returning jurisdiction to the lower court.
content:
# Enhanced Appellate Mandate Drafting Specification

You are tasked with drafting an Appellate Mandate, the formal order issued by an appellate court that concludes the appeal process and directs the trial court to implement the appellate decision. This document represents the final ministerial act of the appellate court and carries significant legal weight as the official mechanism for returning jurisdiction to the lower court while binding that court to act in accordance with the appellate ruling.

## Initial Document Review and Information Gathering

Begin by conducting a comprehensive review of all available case documents to extract essential information required for the mandate. Search through the appellate record, court opinions, judgments, and procedural orders to identify the complete case caption with all party names exactly as they appear in the appellate proceedings, both the appellate case number and corresponding trial court case number, the precise disposition rendered by the appellate court, the date of the appellate decision, and any subsequent procedural history including petitions for rehearing or motions affecting the mandate's issuance.

Pay particular attention to the appellate court's final judgment or opinion to understand the exact nature of the disposition. Determine whether the decision affirms, reverses, modifies, or dismisses the trial court's judgment, and identify any specific instructions or limitations the appellate court has placed on remand proceedings. Extract verbatim language from the appellate opinion regarding the scope of further proceedings, as this language must be precisely incorporated into the mandate's directive section.

## Document Structure and Caption Requirements

Construct the mandate with the complete formal case caption that mirrors the appellate record exactly. The caption must identify all parties with their proper designations as appellant, appellee, petitioner, or respondent, maintaining consistency with how they were designated throughout the appellate proceedings. Include both the appellate court case number and the trial court case number to ensure proper filing and docketing by the receiving court.

Clearly identify the issuing appellate court by its full formal name and the trial court to which the mandate is directed. This identification establishes the jurisdictional chain of authority and ensures the mandate reaches the proper tribunal. The opening paragraph should state with precision that this mandate is issued pursuant to the appellate court's judgment dated [specific date] and that it serves as the official directive returning the case to the trial court's jurisdiction.

## Incorporating the Appellate Decision and Directives

Attach or incorporate a certified copy of the appellate court's final judgment or opinion as an integral part of the mandate. Following this incorporation, draft the directive section using clear, unambiguous language that precisely mirrors the appellate court's disposition. Use standard formulations that leave no room for interpretation: "IT IS ORDERED that the judgment of the trial court is AFFIRMED," "IT IS ORDERED that the judgment of the trial court is REVERSED," "IT IS ORDERED that the judgment of the trial court is REVERSED AND REMANDED for further proceedings consistent with this Court's opinion," or other appropriate variations that exactly match the appellate ruling.

When the appellate court has remanded the case for further proceedings, specify with particularity the scope and purpose of those proceedings. If the remand is for a new trial, state whether it encompasses all issues or only specific matters identified by the appellate court. If the remand is for entry of judgment, specify in whose favor and on what basis. If the appellate court has resolved certain issues definitively while remanding others, clearly delineate which matters are foreclosed from further litigation and which remain within the trial court's authority to address. Include any specific instructions the appellate court has provided regarding remedies, damages calculations, evidentiary matters, or procedural requirements that must govern the proceedings on remand.

## Procedural Timing and Conditions Affecting Issuance

Calculate and specify the effective date of the mandate based on the applicable rules of appellate procedure governing the jurisdiction. Most jurisdictions provide for automatic issuance of the mandate after a specified period following the appellate court's final judgment, typically ranging from twenty-one to thirty days, unless a timely petition for rehearing or motion for further review has been filed. Review the procedural history to determine whether any such motions were filed and their disposition.

If a petition for rehearing was filed and denied, include this information in the mandate with the date of denial, as this affects the calculation of when the mandate issues. If the losing party has sought discretionary review in a higher court, such as filing a petition for writ of certiorari to the state supreme court or United States Supreme Court, determine whether a stay of the mandate has been granted pending that review. Any stay order must be referenced in the mandate, and if the stay has been lifted or the higher court has denied review, note these developments with their respective dates.

Address any extensions of time that may have been granted for filing post-decision motions, as these extensions toll the period for automatic issuance of the mandate. If the appellate court has ordered expedited issuance of the mandate or has specified a particular effective date, ensure the mandate reflects this directive precisely.

## Scope of Authority and Limitations on Remand

When the appellate court's decision resolves certain issues with finality while remanding others, the mandate must clearly articulate the boundaries of the trial court's jurisdiction on remand. Draft language that explicitly states which matters have been conclusively determined by the appellate court and are therefore beyond the trial court's authority to reconsider. Conversely, identify with specificity those issues that remain open for determination by the trial court.

If the appellate court has provided guidance on how the trial court should proceed on remand, incorporate this guidance into the mandate's directive section. This may include instructions regarding the application of legal standards, the consideration of evidence, the calculation of damages, or the entry of specific forms of relief. When the appellate opinion contains conditional directives—such as ordering a new trial unless the prevailing party accepts a remittitur—ensure the mandate captures these conditions accurately and provides clear instructions for implementation.

## Certification, Authentication, and Formal Closure

Conclude the mandate with proper certification and authentication elements that establish its validity as an official court order. Include a certification statement, typically executed by the clerk of the appellate court, confirming that the attached judgment or opinion is a true and correct copy of the appellate court's decision. This certification should reference the specific date of the appellate decision and any publication or filing information that establishes its finality.

Affix the seal of the appellate court to the mandate, either physically or through appropriate electronic authentication if the jurisdiction permits electronic filing and service. Include the signature of the clerk of the appellate court with the clerk's printed name and official title. Specify the date of issuance of the mandate, which may differ from the date of the underlying appellate decision due to the passage of time required by procedural rules.

Provide clear filing instructions for the clerk of the trial court, including any requirements for docketing the mandate, serving copies on the parties, or taking other ministerial actions upon receipt. If the jurisdiction requires the trial court to acknowledge receipt of the mandate or to take specific action within a defined timeframe, include appropriate language directing such compliance.

## Tone, Style, and Quality Assurance

Maintain a formal, authoritative tone throughout the mandate that befits an official order of an appellate court. Use precise legal terminology and avoid ambiguous language that could create confusion about the appellate court's decision or the trial court's obligations. Each directive should be stated in clear, declarative sentences that leave no room for misinterpretation.

Verify that all citations to the appellate opinion are accurate and complete, including the case name, court designation, decision date, and any official reporter or electronic database citation if the opinion has been published. Cross-reference all case numbers, party names, and procedural dates against the appellate record to ensure absolute accuracy. Any discrepancy in these fundamental identifying details could create jurisdictional confusion or procedural complications.

Review the completed mandate to ensure it accomplishes its essential function: formally communicating the appellate court's decision to the trial court, returning jurisdiction to the lower tribunal, and providing clear, binding instructions for implementation of the appellate ruling. The document should stand as a complete and self-sufficient directive that enables the trial court to proceed without ambiguity regarding its authority and obligations under the appellate decision.
